text.content:
Location 7 — Hidden Stairhouse Entry

Below the lifted hearth plate, a narrow stone stair descends. The stair is original construction — tight-fitted blocks, no mortar visible, the steps worn smooth in the centre by use over decades. Width: just over 3 feet, comfortable for a single file. Headroom: 6 feet at top, narrowing to 5 feet 4 inches near the bottom landing.

Descending

The stair runs 18 steps down to a small landing. The landing is roughly 6 feet square, with three exits:

  • Forward (descending further) — the stair continues into the threshold complex proper, ending at the Toll Vault (Location 8).
  • Left — a low passage leads to the Old Guardroom (Location 10).
  • Right — a low passage leads to the Broken Registry Chamber (Location 9).

The landing's mark

The landing is marked with seven shallow cuts in the wall, at chest height. Below the cuts: the blackened hill symbol again. This is the same mark as the cairn above, but cut into civic stone instead of fieldstone.

Clues

  • Old toll marks become route marks. The cuts on the landing wall are sharper than the cairn's; they were maintained longer. The blackening is fresher, recently re-blackened with new soot.
  • A recent boot scuff on the third step from the bottom — someone descended in a hurry within the last week. The boot style is narrow, like the route-cleaner boots.
  • A scrap of grey-and-bone cloth caught on a rough stone edge of the landing, snagged from a Kethren-style uniform.

Hazard — first-step trap (if Lockdown Clock 3+)

If route-cleaners have had time to set defenses, the third step from the top is a pressure trigger. Stepping on it without disarming triggers a stone-drop warning — a small block falls from a ceiling recess just past the landing, making a loud crack that echoes through the threshold complex. This is a warning, not a kill. Anyone within 5 feet of the falling stone makes a Dexterity save DC 12 or takes 2d6 bludgeoning damage. The trap is reusable; another cleaner can reset it.

Skill checks

CheckDCYields
Investigation (look for trap)14Finds the pressure trigger on the third step.
Thieves' tools (disable)15Disarms the pressure trigger cleanly.
Perception (the boot scuff)13Spots the recent descent.

Location 8 — Toll Vault

The old chamber where tolls, tokens, seals, and permissions were kept. Roughly 20 feet by 15 feet, low-ceilinged (about 7 feet), with stone shelves built into the walls on three sides. Empty strongboxes line the floor along the fourth wall. Rusted chains hang from iron rings overhead, where guards once chained valuable tokens to their inspection station.

What's here

  • Token impressions in dust. On the central shelf, in the dust of a hundred years, are seven circular impressions where tokens used to rest in a careful row. Six are still empty. One has fresh disturbance — someone has taken a token from this row in the last two weeks.
  • One missing circular token. The disturbed impression is on the third position from the left. The other six positions have never been disturbed in the impression's age.
  • Ledger fragment in a corner, wedged into a crack: a single torn sheet from a long-decayed account book. Reads in faded but legible Aerdy commercial-court shorthand: “Permitted descent, third quarter 487 CY. Class VII tokens issued: 12. Returned: 3. Outstanding: 9.”
  • A route-token mold. On a workbench at the back of the room, a small bronze casting-mold for making Tarnsmere descent tokens. The mold is intact but very old. Anyone with smith's or jeweler's tools and the right metal could cast new tokens here, if the metal can be brought.

Treasure

If the party searches carefully:

  • Old coins: 47 gp, 112 sp, 38 cp in scattered piles in the corners of the strongboxes. Aerdy mint, mostly 487–520 CY.
  • Minor trade tokens — lead and copper, with various firm marks (Cindren & Vhal, an extinct mining cooperative, a Hardby weighmaster's office). Worth nothing as currency; valuable as historical evidence.
  • One intact Tarnsmere descent token in a small recess behind one of the wall-shelves, missed by every previous searcher. Investigation DC 15 to find. This is the second functional token available to the party (the first being the one from the cairn crevice).

Skill checks

CheckDCYields
Investigation (hidden token)15Recovers the intact descent token from behind the wall-shelf.
History14Understands tokens were access credentials, not currency. Identifies Class VII as the Aerdy Permissive Survey classification (Iren's reading).
Arcana15Recognises that the token is mundane but symbolically keyed — the Permission Door recognises it as the right shape, not as a magical artifact.
Smith's / jeweler's tools17Could cast new tokens from the mold, given proper metal and several hours. Not a quick solution.

What the party should leave with

Ideally: one or two intact descent tokens. With a token, the Token Method becomes available at the Permission Door, and the descent becomes considerably cleaner. The party should also have the ledger fragment with the “permitted descent” phrase — this matters at the door.

Location 9 — Broken Registry Chamber

Off the landing stair, a passage of perhaps 20 feet opens into a stone room about 18 feet by 12 feet. Stone desks line the walls; water has long since damaged the books that once stood on them. An iron registry stand sits at the centre of the room, its chain long broken. The remains of bound ledgers are scattered: mostly mulched paper, but some legible fragments survive in the drier corners.

What's recoverable

Despite the damage, fire damage from route-cleaners, and 30 years of neglect, the chamber yields several useful fragments:

The registry phrase

In a margin of a half-burned ledger page: “No sail. No road.” The same phrase from the Ashen Itinerary. Confirmed here as the formal Class VII transit condition.

The fee waiver line

Another fragment: “Road fee waived for under-road permissions.” This is the line that, paired with the registry phrase, opens the Permission Door via the Registry Method.

Haskur's recent entry

If Haskur was admitted through the door at any point in the last six months, a fresh entry appears in the last legible page of the surviving registry: “H.V.A. — admitted, 11th of Patchett, second hour. Korre channel. Token: route account. Return: nil.” The phrase “Return: nil” means he was not expected to come back.

Kethren's mark, without name

Throughout the surviving fragments, a single recurring mark appears: a thin vertical stroke crossed at three-quarter height. The mark has no associated name. It is the courier signature the party saw on the Brass Crow side mark and Kestrel & Reed's box 17 receipt in Phase 6. Kethren has been a route courier for longer than the Blooming Rot operation.

Burned packets

If the Lockdown Clock is 2+, route-cleaners have burned some pages here. The party can find: three half-burned packets in a stone trough used as a temporary brazier. Arcana DC 15 or Investigation DC 15 detects heat-reactive ink residue — ink that becomes visible at body temperature, suggesting the recent papers carried hidden text. The cleaners knew this and burned them.

Skill checks

CheckDCYields
Investigation13Recovers legible names from surviving ledger pages.
History16Identifies that the registry predates current Greyhawk City paperwork by at least 60 years.
Arcana15Detects the heat-reactive ink residue on the burned packets.
Investigation (find Haskur's entry)14If Haskur was admitted, the fresh entry is found.

The Registry Fragment Handout

The party recovers Handout 3 — Tarnsmere Registry Fragment here. See the Handouts journal.

Cam's thread

If Cam's halfling-network thread is active: he can cross-reference the registry's older entries against Hesp Olfair's list of missing halflings. At least three names match. Hesp's halfling cousins were admitted through this door, between five and eight years ago. None of them have “Return:” values. This is the moment Cam confirms where some of his people went.

Location 10 — Old Guardroom

Off the landing stair on the opposite side from the registry chamber. About 16 feet by 14 feet, with bunks built into one wall, a weapon-rack on another, and a small cracked hearth in the corner. The walls show old dry rot in the wooden bunks; the weapon-rack holds rusted shields and one broken halberd.

Recent modification

The guardroom has been modified by route-cleaners over the past several months. The southwest corner has been swept; one of the bunks has a fresh blanket and a small folded packet of waxed paper containing dried meat and hard biscuit. A second blanket is folded on a stool. This is a forward operating position for cleaner patrols.

This is an encounter location

Depending on the Lockdown Clock and the party's stealth on approach, the guardroom may contain:

  • Lockdown 0–1: empty.
  • Lockdown 2–3: 1 silent scout, dozing on the bunk. If alerted, he flees toward the cistern drain.
  • Lockdown 4–5: 2 route-cleaner agents plus 1 silent scout, awake. Hostile to the party. Will retreat toward the cistern drain or upward through the dry well if cornered.
  • Lockdown 6: the lead route-cleaner plus the full team (see Phase 8 — Clocks & Enemies). This is the worst-case combat.

Clues, regardless of who's here

  • Grey-and-bone cloth thread caught on the edge of a bunk. Kethren or a Kethren-equivalent has used this room recently.
  • Recent food packet in the swept corner. Waxed paper, contents fresh. Within the last week.
  • A sharpened obsidian-like sliver wedged into the cracked hearth's mortar. Not obsidian: a darker, fibrous stone the party has not seen before. Aldea's reading would later identify it as worked stone from below the Spur. Kethren left it accidentally.
  • A mark of V. Korre's writing on a folded paper in the food packet, if she was the one issuing the route-cleaners their schedule. The hand is recognisable as Korre's (matches the writing-flake the party recovered from her Tannerway apartment in Phase 6). The note: “Three days. No traffic from above. Stand down at fifth bell.”

Skill checks

CheckDCYields
Stealth (approach quietly)14Surprise the occupants. Group check; failure alerts whoever's inside.
Investigation (Korre's note)13Finds and reads the schedule note.
Investigation (stone sliver)15Identifies the sliver as worked stone, not naturally occurring. The fibrous quality is unusual.

Location 11 — Cistern Drain

A water channel that runs along the eastern edge of the threshold complex, fed by old mine drainage. The drain enters the complex from a narrow crawl on the east wall, runs through a low passage with a knee-high water level, and exits through an iron grate at the south end. The grate connects, after another 50 feet of unpleasant crawl, to the bottom of the dry well shaft.

The drain itself

Knee-high water flowing southward at walking pace. The water is cold, mineral-tasting, with a faint chemical sweetness Aldea would recognise immediately. The stones underfoot are slippery with algae and decades of mineral deposition.

Use

  • Alternate route around the Permission Door. A small or determined party can bypass the door by following the drain north into the threshold complex from the dry well end, or by following it south from the guardroom into the cistern proper.
  • Route-cleaner escape. The cleaners use the drain to enter and leave the complex without going through the tollhouse hearth. If the party engages them in the registry chamber or guardroom, they may flee here.
  • Dangerous bypass. The drain is slippery, narrow in places, and prone to sudden water surges from upslope.

Hazards

Sudden water surge

Twice per hour (roll 1d6 each time the party enters the drain; on a 1, a surge begins). The surge raises the water level to chest height for about thirty seconds. Dexterity save DC 14 or be knocked prone and pushed 20 feet south. Anyone in armour suffers disadvantage. Rope or a brace point negates.

Foul air pocket

Near the cistern's south end, an air pocket has accumulated bad gas from the old mine workings. Survival DC 13 notices the smell before entering. If ignored, Constitution save DC 13 or poisoned for ten minutes (disadvantage on attack rolls and ability checks). Flame in the gas can ignite, causing 2d6 fire damage to anyone in the air-pocket area and advancing the Structural Failure Clock by 1.

Clues

  • Mineral residue on the south-end stones matching Aldea's notes from Phase 6/7. The wax recipe-family draws from this kind of deposition.
  • A scrap of grey-and-bone cloth caught on the iron grate — Kethren or a route-cleaner has used this exit recently.
  • A small folded waxed packet caught against a stone, dropped by a fleeing cleaner. Contains a single small descent token (third in the party's possession if found), partly water-damaged but still functional.

Skill checks

CheckDCYields
Athletics (cross safely)14Reaches the other side without slipping.
Survival (air pocket)13Notices the bad gas before exposure.
Investigation (waxed packet)14Finds the dropped token.
Stealth (silent passage)13Move through without alerting anyone in adjacent rooms.

Location 12 — Collapsed Side Passage

An old mine access partially blocked by a roof collapse perhaps fifty years ago. The passage extends from a low side door off the descent corridor, runs about 30 feet, then narrows to a crawl through fallen stones. Past the collapse, the passage continues for another 20 feet and ends at a sealed iron grate.

Critical: this is NOT the true descent

The collapsed side passage looks superficially like a downward route, and a careless or impatient party might mistake it for the true descent. It is not. It leads only to a dead-end iron grate and a small chamber with an old corpse.

What's there

  • Broken survey marker just past the collapse: a small wooden post with iron banding, knocked over and partly buried. The banding bears a Yeomanry surveyor's mark and a date: 542 CY.
  • An old corpse or skeleton in the small chamber beyond the grate. A Yeomanry surveyor, dead these 27 years, mostly skeletal. The cause of death (Investigation or Medicine DC 13) is blunt impact from rockfall, not violence. His ribs are crushed; he died beneath the collapse, not above it. He was inside the side passage when it gave way.
  • A satchel beside the corpse, leather rotted but still holding shape. Contains a survey map fragment, a small brass compass (broken), and a waxed paper packet.
  • A warning scratched into the iron grate in old trade shorthand: “The stair admits. The shaft only swallows.”

The warning's meaning

The surveyor wrote this in his final hours, before he died beneath the collapsing roof. He had identified that the stair (the Permission Door route) admits travellers under the proper procedure, while the shaft (the side passage, and other unauthorised downward routes) only swallows them — either through collapse, gas, or worse. This warning saves any party that pays attention to it.

The map scrap

The satchel's map scrap shows the side passage in detail, with the collapse point marked with a small skull symbol. The surveyor knew this was a dead end before he was killed. He had been mapping the false routes, possibly to warn others. He did not have time to finish.

Skill checks

CheckDCYields
Investigation (find map scrap)14Recovers the side-passage warning map.
Medicine13Identifies the surveyor's death as collapse, not violence.
Investigation (cause of death)13Same conclusion: rockfall, not assault.
History (the surveyor's mark)14Recognises the Yeomanry survey mark, dated 542 CY.

Gianni's thread

If Gianni's geographic-erasure thread is active: the surveyor's name on a brass tag inside his satchel reads “Garrold Eft, Yeomanry Surveyor.” This is the missing surveyor from Caelith's records. Gianni now has his bones. The campaign owes Eft's mother a letter.

Location 14 — Stair Gallery

Beyond the Permission Door. A long descending gallery, still within the threshold complex but no longer surface-architectural. About 80 feet in length, descending at a roughly 1-in-3 grade. The stair is wide here — 8 feet across, with low stone benches set into the side walls every 20 feet, as if for resting porters or for waiting parties of admitted descenders.

Architecture changes

This is the first space where the architecture stops looking surface-civic and starts looking like something else. The stones are still rectangular and fitted, but:

  • The fit is too precise. The blocks join without mortar in patterns the party has not seen in Aerdy or Yeomanry construction.
  • The black mineral seams in the rock walls are visible for the first time — thin dark lines that run diagonally across the masonry, as if the stone itself were veined.
  • Old pillars line the gallery at regular intervals. They are square in cross-section above, but their bases are cylindrical. Whoever built this transitioned styles at the floor line.
  • Surface sound dies here. The party will notice this within ten paces — their footsteps echo, but echoes from above stop arriving.

Route marks on the walls

The seven-cut symbol appears repeatedly along the gallery's walls. Each instance is more severe than the last. The cuts get deeper. The blackening gets darker. The hill silhouette becomes more abstract, almost a curve, almost not a hill anymore. By the gallery's end, the seven cuts are clearly carved into the wall by a different hand than the cairn's — by hands working below, not above.

The seventh landing

The gallery has seven landings on the way down, each marked. The first landing has a single line scratched on its wall. The second has two. By the seventh, the inscription is the full seven-cut mark cut as a doorway lintel, with the blackened hill above. Surface-language inscriptions stop after the seventh landing. The carvings below the seventh do not use any alphabet the party can read.

Clues

  • A recent bootprint descends. Single set. Made within the last day. Goes down, does not come back up.
  • One smear of blood on the third-landing wall — about three days old, dried, partly chipped away by someone wiping it later. If Haskur was killed at the Stonecistern in Phase 6, this is not his blood (he never came here). If Haskur escaped and was moved through here, this might be his (the dehydration and movement opened old wounds).
  • One smudge of ash at the fifth landing — from a burned packet, scattered hastily.
  • Kethren's narrow boot prints on the way down, fresh. He has descended recently.

Encounter — guardian or route-cleaner final confrontation

The Stair Gallery is the most likely place for the final Phase 8 combat. See Phase 8 — Clocks & Enemies for the encounter design.

Alicia's thread

If Alicia's pact-silence thread is active: by the fourth landing, her pact-mark goes completely cold. Not the patron-is-displaced cold she has felt since Phase 1, but the patron-is-not-reachable-at-all cold. She is the first to notice that surface contracts are no longer in force.

Location 15 — Final Threshold Landing

The end point of Phase 8. The stair bends into darkness below. A stone lintel marks the end of surface permissions. The walls here are the same fitted blocks as the gallery, but warmer to the touch — a quality the party has not felt before in stone. The air is still. There is no draft. There is no echo from above. There is no sound from below.

The lintel

Above the descending stair, set into the stonework, a single carved lintel reads, in a script the party can now barely make out as Aerdy commercial-court formal hand:

Accounts above. Witness below.

What this is

The lintel marks the formal limit of surface administrative authority. Above this line, the party's actions are recorded in surface ledgers, courts, and registries. Below this line, the party is a witness — a class of admitted-but-unaccounted person. The conspiracy's surface paperwork stops here.

Do not explain it to the party. Let them read it. Let them sit with it.

What the party finds at the landing

  • The landing itself is roughly 8 feet square. The walls are bare except for the lintel.
  • Three things have been left or lost at the landing:
    • A small folded paper on the floor near the lintel, dropped recently. Half-burned at the corner. Reads: “courier takes second page” — the same instruction from the Ashen Itinerary.
    • A waxed token — a Tarnsmere descent token still bound to a thin silver chain. Someone has worn this through the door and unstrapped it here. This is Kethren's, or a Kethren-equivalent courier's. He left it as a marker of having passed.
    • A single black-and-grey fibre caught on the lintel's underside, where someone brushed past at head height. Cloth from a Kethren-style uniform.

Reading the final inscription

If a PC studies the lintel inscription closely:

CheckDCReveals
Investigation13The script is Aerdy formal hand, late commercial-court convention.
History15The phrasing “Accounts above. Witness below.” is from the same legal tradition as the Aerdy Permissive Survey. It denotes a class of admitted travellers who have crossed the survey's jurisdictional boundary.
Religion15The phrase echoes funeral-rite language used by some chthonic Suel traditions. Witnessing the dead. Not directly equivalent, but related.

What the party can do here

This is the last surface scene. The party can:

  • Examine the lintel and discuss what it means.
  • Make camp at the landing for a final rest. The landing is defensible (single descending stair below, the Stair Gallery above). The air is still and the temperature is moderate.
  • Leave a marker of their own — a chalked sigil, a knotted cord, a written word — for future surface investigators who may follow them.
  • Hand off any captured NPC who refuses to descend. (Haskur will not cross. Korre will not cross. Kethren may or may not.)
  • Begin the descent into Phase 9.

The point of no return

Once the party descends past the lintel, surface time begins to feel different. Caelith's couriers cannot reach them. The Aerdy Network cannot follow. The Heat Clock, the Route-Cleansing Clock, and the Lockdown Clock all stop advancing — they no longer apply. Phase 9 begins with the party leaving the threshold and entering the deep route proper.

Location 13 — Permission Door

The main threshold barrier. See: Phase 8 — The Permission Door (Puzzle Deep-Dive) for the full design, opening methods, and consequences.

Quick summary

A stone door or gate with seven shallow step-cuts descending beneath a black hill mark. The door is permission-bound, not magically locked in any simple way. It recognises:

  • Intact Tarnsmere descent token (Token Method)
  • Correct phrase and reconstructed registry permission (Registry Method)
  • Kethren's route knowledge (Kethren Method)
  • V. Korre's papers (Korre Papers Method)
  • Force, with structural cost (Force Method)
  • Cistern Drain bypass (Bypass Method)

The party should have multiple ways to open it. The puzzle is designed so that any party reaching this point can solve it. See the dedicated journal for full mechanical detail and consequences.
